title: Chapter 20: Blood and Ash
paragraphs: Lord Valtor's footsteps across the perfect jade of the White Hall were absolutely silent, but everyone present felt them like the strikes of an icy hammer on an anvil. With his appearance, the scent of incense vanished, replaced by a subtle, metallic aroma of old blood and a frosty winter night. The High Elders, who for centuries had relished their perceived greatness, noticeably shrank on their carved thrones.

Valtor stopped a few paces from Yashil. Tall, clad in strict black velvet, he looked like the living embodiment of the very death that the elves so desperately tried to ignore. His eyes, the color of dark ruby, swept inquiringly over the figure of the small green-skinned being, whose blood still slowly dripped onto the flawless floor.

"The High Elves have written treatises on honor for centuries, goblin," Valtor's voice was soft, almost velvety, but it radiated centuries of cold. "They elevated pride to an absolute. But when the heavens began to rot, their pride turned into fear. And you... you stand here, bleeding, and offer them your life. For what? Are you seeking a beautiful death in this fading world?"
Yashil calmly met the gaze of the immortal predator. In the vampire's eyes, he saw neither contempt nor hatred—only the infinite, icy weariness of a being that had outlived too many eras.

"There is no such thing as a beautiful death, milord," Yashil replied quietly. "There is only death that has meaning, and death that is devoid of it. The Void will sooner or later consume us all. You, and the Elders, and Rotten Log. I didn't come to save the world. I came to prove that even in the face of the absolute end, we can choose who we are to be. Monsters tearing each other's throats for an extra breath of air... or those who stand shoulder to shoulder."
Valtor was silent for a long time. Not a muscle moved on his pale, aristocratic face, but in the depths of his ruby eyes, a spark of sincere, deep interest flared.

Behind Yashil, Kirah growled gutturally, on the edge of audibility. The demon felt the nature of the vampire lord with all his black, pitchy being. It was not the fierce magic of the Void, but a cold, calculating power of necromancy. Valtor turned his gaze to the titan.
"A child of Zakhan hiding his claws behind the back of a goblin," the corners of Valtor's lips lifted slightly. "An amusing paradox. Your creator would have torn out your heart for such weakness."
"My heart belongs to me," Kirah answered hoarsely, like shifting tectonic plates. "And I would rather let it stop than become a blind tool again."
Renée, who had been watching the scene with light interest until then, elegantly released a thin stream of smoke and flicked away the ash.

"An aristocratic blood-drinker with a philosophical bent. How predictable and yet refreshing compared to these dusty old men," the fox tilted her head slightly, her amber eyes meeting Valtor's. "You, lord, obviously have this city by the throat. Why this performance with us?"

Valtor smirked. It was the smile of two predators who had instantly recognized each other. "A fox playing at nihilism. We all wear masks, my dear. But my mask at least allows this city not to collapse."

"Valtor!" the voice of High Elder Elarion broke into a screech, violating the icy aesthetics of the moment. The elder rose from his throne, pointing a shaking finger at the vampire. "You are crossing the line! We tolerate your presence and your dead only because we had a pact! But I will not allow you to have social chats with this menagerie in the sacred White Hall!"
Valtor turned slowly, gracefully toward the dais. The temperature in the hall dropped several more degrees. The magical spheres under the dome trembled and almost went out.

"You tolerate my presence, Elarion?" the vampire's voice became quiet, but in that silence lurked the threat of a falling avalanche. "My legions of the Undying are right now holding the Northern Breach, laying their bodies into the gaps left by your magic. My blood powers the runes on the outer ring while you waste precious energy on keeping your robes white. Do not dare speak to me of tolerance. You are dust that someone forgot to sweep away."
The Elders huddled in their chairs. Valtor turned back to Yashil, his face instantly regaining its former composure.

"There is a place in this city that the elves prefer not to notice," the vampire said, looking at the goblin. "The Gates of Lamentation. The southeast sector of the outer ring. The magical shields there have long since thinned, and the concentration of radioactive ash is such that an ordinary sentry goes mad within a couple of weeks. Those they want to write off are sent there. A breach of the Void is expected there any day now."
Janavar, who had been lazily picking at the joint between marble slabs with a claw, raised his ears.

"Sounds like an ideal place for those with nothing to lose," the werewolf chuckled. "You know what they say? A wolf isn't afraid of the dark, because he is part of that dark himself. Give us these gates, blood-drinker. We'll show you how real predators howl. Auf."
Valtor nodded slightly to the werewolf, accepting his boldness.

"I give you this sector, Yashil of Rotten Log," the lord said, looking into the goblin's dark eyes. "The Elders will give you neither provisions nor elven weapons. You will go there as outcasts. If you run—the guard will shoot you in the back. If you die—you will prove Elarion right. But if you stand..."
Valtor paused, allowing the silence to fill the hall.
"If you stand, you will become a precedent. You will prove to this fading world that the spirit does not depend on race."
Yashil did not hesitate for a second. He slowly bowed his head—not in a sign of slavish submission, but as a sign of an equal contract.
"We take the Gates of Lamentation, Lord Valtor," the goblin replied.
Kirah behind him exhaled heavily, and in that sound was heard a long-awaited relief. Renée chuckled quietly, hiding the mouthpiece, and Janavar clanged his axe onto his broad shoulder.
When the four outcasts turned and headed for the exit of the White Hall, not a single elder dared to utter a word. Their steps echoed under the vaults of the tomb of illusions.

They stepped back out into the city, under the leaden, ash-crying sky of Agdash. Ahead of them lay the most hopeless defensive line in the world. But for the first time in his life, Yashil felt that he was not walking into a void. He was walking toward his destiny.

And somewhere on other tiers of this huge, rotting megalopolis, fate was already weaving the threads, preparing their meeting with the one whose black flame had long needed such a light. With the fire elf Avotu.
